title: Expectation Pauli-Lubanski vector and intrinsic angular momentum of relativistic wavepackets
abstract: In non-relativistic mechanics, the total (orbital) angular momentum (AM) of a spatially-distributed system can be decomposed into intrinsic and extrinsic contributions. In relativistic quantum mechanics, intrinsic AM is typically associated with spin, which can be described using the Pauli-Lubanski four-vector. Here, we develop a unified formalism that combines the main features of both approaches and describes the intrinsic AM of a relativistic wavepacket, including both spin and orbital contributions. Our approach is based on the "expectation Pauli-Lubanski vector" constructed from the expectation values of the wavepacket's momentum and AM. Equivalently, it defines the intrinsic AM relative to the wavepacket's energy centroid. In contrast to the conventional Pauli-Lubanski formalism, the zero-mass singularity does not occur for the expectation Pauli-Lubanski vector. Consequently, the intrinsic AM of a wavepacket may have an arbitrary orientation with respect to its momentum, even for massless particles. We illustrate the general theory with a number of examples of relativistic wave beams and packets carrying spin and orbital AM.
summary: The paper introduces a new formalism using the expectation Pauli-Lubanski vector to describe the intrinsic angular momentum of relativistic wavepackets. This approach successfully unifies spin and orbital contributions and avoids the mathematical singularities encountered in standard theory for massless particles. It demonstrates that even massless wavepackets can possess intrinsic angular momentum with orientations arbitrary to their direction of motion.
main_problem: The difficulty in providing a unified relativistic description of the intrinsic angular momentum of wavepackets that incorporates both spin and orbital contributions, and resolving the singularity of the standard Pauli-Lubanski formalism for massless particles.
main_result: The development of an 'expectation Pauli-Lubanski vector' that removes the zero-mass singularity and allows the intrinsic angular momentum of a wavepacket to have an arbitrary orientation relative to its momentum, even for massless particles.
method: A new theoretical framework based on the expectation values of momentum and angular momentum operators, using the energy centroid as a reference point for the decomposition of angular momentum.
model_or_system: Relativistic wavepackets and wave beams (including massive and massless particles) with structured phase profiles, such as Bessel beams and spatiotemporal vortex pulses.
key_observables: Expectation Pauli-Lubanski vector, intrinsic and extrinsic angular momentum, spin, orbital angular momentum, and effective mass.
important_parameters: Mass regimes (massive vs. massless), paraxial vs. non-paraxial regimes, and Lorentz boost velocity.
main_assumptions: The approach assumes the Hermitian symmetrization of the operator rE and omits the spin contribution to the boost momentum for simplicity; the decomposition is not Lorentz-covariant.
figures_summary: Figure 5 shows a 2D Bessel beam in its rest frame and its deformed, Lorentz-contracted state after a transverse Lorentz boost.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of angular momentum decomposition, proposes the expectation Pauli-Lubanski vector formalism, demonstrates its ability to handle massless particles without singularities, and illustrates the theory using examples like Bessel beams and spatiotemporal vortices.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant to quantum optics and structured light, as it provides a rigorous relativistic foundation for understanding how spin and orbital angular momentum behave in moving, localized light beams.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. (a) Interference of two plane electromagnetic waves with different wavevectors and opposite helicities (shown in magenta and cyan) yields a field with the mean spin (intrinsic AM) ⟨S⟩orthogonal to the mean momentum ⟨p⟩[6, 23, 24]. (b) A Lorentz boost with velocity u = ⟨p⟩/⟨E⟩transforms this field to its rest frame, where ⟨p0⟩= 0 and the field be- comes a standing wave with the mean spin ⟨S0⟩= γ⟨S⟩.

FIG. 1. Transverse Lorentz boost with velocity u = 0.6ˆx applied to a z-propagating circularly-polarized plane electro- magnetic wave (shown here for helicity λ = 1). The momen- tum, spin, and polarization are shown by the blue, red, and magenta arrows, respectively. The spin remains aligned with the momentum, see Eqs. (16) and (31).

FIG. 3. (a) Plane-wave spectrum of a Bessel beam with kr = 0.4k, arbitrary m, and azimuthal phase winding cor- responding to ℓ= 2 (encoded by hue colors). The expec- tation values of the momentum and intrinsic AM are shown by the large blue and red arrows, respectively. (b) Lorentz boost to the rest frame (u = ˆzkz/ω), where the mean mo- mentum vanishes, while the intrinsic AM remains unchanged, see Eqs. (35) and (37). (c,d) Corresponding transverse spa- tial distributions of the scalar wavefunction ψ(r) (obtained by Fourier transform of the plane-wave spectra). Brightness and hue colors represent intensity and phase, respectively. The distributions are identical in the two frames. The...

The total, intrinsic, and extrinsic AM, given by Eqs. (40)–(42), are shown by the magenta, red, and yellow arrows, respectively. The intrin- sic AM is aligned with the momentum only in the massless case, m = 0. (c,d) Transverse spatial distributions of the corresponding wave fields in the original and boosted frames. Brightness and hue colors represent the energy density and phase, respectively (for simplicity, the energy density was de- termined for the scalar Klein-Gordon field, as in [25, 41]). FIG. 5. (a) A 2D Bessel beam in its rest frame, with kr = k = 1.33m and ℓ= 2. (b) Lorentz boost with velocity u = 0.7ˆx. The total, intrinsic, and extrinsic AM, described by Eqs. (47) and (48), are shown by the magenta, red, and yellow arrows, respectively. The intrinsic AM is orthogonal to the mean momentum, independently of the mass m. (c,d) Transverse spatial distributions of the corresponding wave fields in the rest and boosted frames. Brightness and hue colors represent the energy density and phase, respectively. The transverse shift of the energy centroid, responsible for the extrinsic AM, is indicated in (d).

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The new paper's central result—the unification of spin and orbital angular momentum in relativistic wavepackets—directly addresses the coupling between internal (spin) and external (momentum/orbital) degrees of freedom.
